---
title: "Discrete geometry: meshes, volumes, and the operators on them"
---

Neuroimaging geometry lives in two discrete representations - volumes (voxel grids: images, masks,
distance fields) and surfaces (triangle meshes: the cortical sheet). Each is a finite approximation
of a smooth continuous object: a region of space, a curved 2-manifold. This unit builds the
operators on them - Laplacians, curvature, distance, isosurfaces - from intuition to rigour, and
shows that almost all of the work is done by two ideas: one semiring operator-machine (a
gather-and-reduce) that becomes diffusion, dilation, or a distance transform purely by swapping its
arithmetic, and one Laplace--Beltrami operator $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ that drives smoothing, curvature,
and spectra. A third idea, running underneath both, is that each operator is built to respect the
*symmetries* of the object it acts on - which is what makes it the right geometric tool and, in the
learned setting, the right inductive bias.

This unit is written for a reader fluent in linear algebra - matrices, least squares,
eigendecomposition, transposes, rank - but with no formal training in differential geometry,
numerical methods, or graphics. We build every operator up from the linear algebra you already own,
and we explain each geometric idea the first time it appears. It is the sibling of the
[statistical modelling suite](../statistics/index.qmd) and shares its house style. This page is the
map; a chapter-per-topic ladder follows.

## The governing idea {#sec-governing}

A triangle mesh is not a surface; a voxel grid is not a region of space. Each is a discretisation -
a finite stand-in for a smooth continuous object we can never store exactly. The cortical sheet is a
smooth 2-manifold; we hold it as a few hundred thousand triangles. A brain mask is a smooth solid
region; we hold it as a grid of 1-mm cubes. The whole craft of this unit is to compute a geometric
quantity - a Laplacian, a curvature, a distance - *on the discrete object* in such a way that, as the
discretisation is refined, the answer converges to the continuous truth.

That single sentence sets three standards we hold every routine to.

1. **Discrete approximates smooth.** Every operator is the discrete shadow of a continuous one (a
   finite-difference stencil approximates $\partial^2/\partial x^2$; the cotangent Laplacian
   approximates the Laplace--Beltrami operator $\Delta$ on the manifold). We always name the
   continuous object being approximated.
2. **Convergence under refinement.** A good discrete operator is not merely "close enough at the
   resolution you happen to have"; its error shrinks as the mesh is subdivided or the grid is made
   finer. Where the rate is known, we state it.
3. **Validation by oracle and correlation, not bit-parity.** We do not check a geometry routine by
   demanding it match a reference implementation bit-for-bit - two correct codes will disagree in the
   last few bits. We check it against an analytic oracle (a sphere of radius $r$ has mean curvature
   $H = 1/r$ everywhere; the Gauss--Bonnet theorem fixes $\sum_i K_i A_i = 2\pi \chi$ for a closed
   surface) and against real data by class and correlation (does it land in the right regime, and
   does it track a trusted tool like ITK with high correlation?). This agreement in *behaviour*, not
   in *bits*, runs through the whole unit.

Hold these three together and the unit has a spine: every chapter introduces a discrete operator,
names its continuous limit, and tells you how to know it is right.

## The two representations and the bridge {#sec-bridge}

Everything here is one of two shapes, and a single round trip connects them.

**Volumes.** A scalar field sampled on a regular grid: an intensity image, a binary mask, a
probability map, or a (signed-)distance field $s(x)$ that is negative inside an object and zero on
its boundary. Coordinates are index space (grid index $i$ to position $i$), arrays are
channels-last, and multi-axis grids use `indexing='ij'`. Volume operators - morphology, the Euclidean
distance transform, the SDF - live in [Volumes](volumes.qmd).

**Surfaces.** A triangle mesh $(V, F)$: vertices $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times 3}$ (rows $v_i$) and
faces $F \in \mathbb{Z}^{m\times 3}$ (triples of vertex indices). The cortical sheet is the canonical
example. Mesh operators - the cotangent Laplacian, curvature, the spectrum - span
[Laplace--Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd) through [Curvature](curvature.qmd).

**The bridge.** The two representations convert into each other through the zero level set of a
signed-distance field. Given a volume, `marching_cubes`
([volume-surface](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching)) walks the grid and stitches together the polygon
where $s$ crosses the isovalue, producing a mesh. Given a mesh, `mesh_to_sdf`
([volume-surface](volume-surface.qmd#sec-mesh-sdf)) rasterises it back to a signed-distance volume by
computing, at every voxel, the distance to the nearest triangle. The two are inverses up to
discretisation error, and the round trip is the workhorse behind mask-to-surface and surface-repair
pipelines.

@fig-index-bridge runs the round trip in 2D, where it is easy to see: an analytic SDF, the contour
`marching_cubes` would extract (here its 2D cousin, marching-squares), and the SDF you recover by
rasterising that contour back.

```{python}
#| label: fig-index-bridge
#| fig-cap: "The volume ↔ surface bridge. Left: an analytic signed-distance field s(x), negative (blue) inside, zero on the black contour. Middle: the s=0 level set extracted by marching-squares (the 2D sibling of marching_cubes). Right: rasterising that contour back to a signed-distance volume — the round trip closes up to discretisation error."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- An analytic signed-distance field: union of two discs (negative inside) ---
# We work on a 2D slice; the same idea lifts verbatim to 3D voxels.
n = 200
xs = np.linspace(-1.5, 1.5, n)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, xs, indexing="ij")

def disc_sdf(cx, cy, r):
    return np.sqrt((X - cx) ** 2 + (Y - cy) ** 2) - r

# union of SDFs is the elementwise min
s = np.minimum(disc_sdf(-0.35, 0.0, 0.7), disc_sdf(0.45, 0.1, 0.55))

# --- Marching squares: extract the s = 0 contour by linear edge interpolation ---
# For each grid edge whose endpoints straddle 0, place a vertex where s crosses 0.
def marching_squares(field, gx, gy):
    segs = []
    inside = field < 0.0
    for i in range(field.shape[0] - 1):
        for j in range(field.shape[1] - 1):
            # the 4 corners of cell (i,j) going CCW
            corners = [(i, j), (i + 1, j), (i + 1, j + 1), (i, j + 1)]
            code = sum((1 << k) for k, c in enumerate(corners) if inside[c])
            if code in (0, 15):
                continue
            # crossing point on an edge between corners a,b
            pts = []
            for a, b in [(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 0)]:
                ca, cb = corners[a], corners[b]
                sa, sb = field[ca], field[cb]
                if (sa < 0) != (sb < 0):
                    t = sa / (sa - sb)
                    px = gx[ca[0]] + t * (gx[cb[0]] - gx[ca[0]])
                    py = gy[ca[1]] + t * (gy[cb[1]] - gy[ca[1]])
                    pts.append((px, py))
            # connect the (usually 2) crossing points
            for k in range(0, len(pts) - 1, 2):
                segs.append((pts[k], pts[k + 1]))
    return segs

segs = marching_squares(s, xs, xs)

# --- Rasterise the extracted contour back to an SDF: distance to nearest segment ---
# (the mesh -> SDF leg of the round trip; we use segment midpoints as a proxy)
mids = np.array([((a[0] + b[0]) / 2, (a[1] + b[1]) / 2) for a, b in segs])
P = np.stack([X.ravel(), Y.ravel()], axis=1)
d = np.min(np.linalg.norm(P[:, None, :] - mids[None, :, :], axis=2), axis=1)
s_back = d.reshape(X.shape) * np.where(s < 0, -1.0, 1.0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))
ax[0].contourf(X, Y, s, levels=20, cmap="RdBu")
ax[0].contour(X, Y, s, levels=[0], colors="k", linewidths=1.5)
ax[0].set_title("volume: analytic SDF $s(x)$\n(negative inside)")
for a, b in segs:
    ax[1].plot([a[0], b[0]], [a[1], b[1]], color="k", lw=1.0)
ax[1].set_xlim(-1.5, 1.5); ax[1].set_ylim(-1.5, 1.5)
ax[1].set_title("surface: marching-squares\ncontour of $s=0$")
ax[2].contourf(X, Y, s_back, levels=20, cmap="RdBu")
ax[2].contour(X, Y, s_back, levels=[0], colors="k", linewidths=1.5)
ax[2].set_title("back to volume:\nrasterised SDF")
for a in ax:
    a.set_aspect("equal"); a.set_xticks([]); a.set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle("The volume <-> surface bridge: a round trip through the s=0 level set", y=1.02)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The 3D routines, `marching_cubes` and `mesh_to_sdf`, are both **HOST-CTOR**: their output shape is
data-dependent (you cannot know the triangle count until you have walked the grid), so they are
host-side NumPy builders that emit JAX arrays rather than fixed-shape jittable kernels - never put
either inside a `jit`-ed loop. `nitrix`'s `marching_cubes` uses marching *tetrahedra* (a
Freudenthal--Kuhn split of each cube into six tets) rather than the classic Lorensen--Cline cube
table, precisely because the tetrahedral version is *watertight and manifold by construction* - a
property that matters for the genus-0 goal discussed in
[volume-surface](volume-surface.qmd#sec-topology).

## The unifying threads {#sec-threads}

Four ideas recur on nearly every page. Meet them once here; each chapter develops its own.

### Thread 1 — symmetry, invariance, and inductive bias {#sec-thread-symmetry}

The deepest organizing principle of the unit is the one Felix Klein placed at the centre of
geometry: a geometry is defined by the group of transformations under which its meaningful quantities
are unchanged. Choosing which transformations should preserve meaning is choosing the geometry - and,
for an algorithm built on it, choosing the inductive bias. Two precise notions recur. A quantity is
*invariant* to a transformation $g$ if $q(g\cdot x) = q(x)$; a map is *equivariant* if transforming
the input transforms the output correspondingly, $F(g\cdot x) = \rho(g)\,F(x)$ (invariance is the
special case where the output does not move).

These are not decorations. They decide what an operator is allowed to compute, and a well-chosen
invariance is a strong prior that shrinks the hypothesis space and makes predictions transform
correctly on inputs never seen. The principle is built into the operators rather than learned from
data: a mesh operator built from adjacency, lengths, angles, and areas is *permutation equivariant*
(it does not depend on vertex ordering); a grid stencil is *translation equivariant* (the same rule
applies everywhere); a spherical convolution respects the rotation group of the sphere. The arc runs
through the whole unit - it is defined in [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-transformations-bias),
the cotangent Laplacian is shown intrinsic (isometry-invariant) in
[Laplace--Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent), curvature splits into the extrinsic $H$ and
the isometry-invariant $K$ (Theorema Egregium) in [Curvature](curvature.qmd#sec-gaussian), the
Laplacian spectrum is an isometry-invariant Shape-DNA in [Spectral](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen), the
Euler characteristic is invariant under *any* homeomorphism in
[volume-surface](volume-surface.qmd#sec-topology), and permutation equivariance is the defining bias
of graph operators in [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-graph-laplacian). Read this thread and
the unit reads as one argument: the right discrete operator is the one whose symmetries match the
geometry's.

### Thread 2 — the semiring machine {#sec-thread-semiring}

A surprising number of geometry operators are the *same computation* - visit each element's
neighbours, combine the gathered values, reduce them to one number - run over *different arithmetic*.
Abstract the arithmetic into a semiring: a pair $(\oplus, \otimes)$ of a reduce and a combine, with
an identity for $\oplus$. Then:

- **REAL** $(\oplus, \otimes) = (+, \times)$ gives the ordinary weighted sum: one step of *diffusion
  / smoothing*;
- **max-plus** $(\max, +)$ gives *dilation* - morphological growth;
- **min-plus** $(\min, +)$ gives a relaxation of the *distance transform* - shortest path under
  additive edge costs.

This connects directly to Thread 1: the gather fixes which neighbours interact (a fixed local
symmetry - translation on a grid, the 1-ring on a mesh), and the semiring fixes how their evidence
combines (the prior on what the operation means). @fig-index-semiring runs one tiny
gather-and-reduce loop with three different $(\oplus, \otimes)$ pairs and gets three different
geometric behaviours out of it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-index-semiring
#| fig-cap: "One operator machine, three semirings. The same gather-and-reduce over a 3-tap neighbourhood becomes diffusion under REAL (+,×), dilation under max-plus (max,+), and a distance transform under min-plus (min,+). The grey object is identical in all three panels; only the arithmetic changed."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# One discrete signal on a line; one 3-tap stencil; three different (reduce, combine)
# pairs run the SAME gather-and-reduce machine to do three different geometric jobs.
n = 60
x = np.arange(n)
mask = ((x > 18) & (x < 30)) | ((x > 38) & (x < 44))  # a binary "object"
mask = mask.astype(float)

# 3-tap neighbourhood stencil (offsets -1, 0, +1) with structuring weights.
offsets = np.array([-1, 0, 1])
w_diffuse = np.array([0.25, 0.5, 0.25])  # REAL: (+, *) weighted average
w_struct = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])     # tropical: flat structuring element

def gather(sig, off):
    idx = np.clip(np.arange(n)[:, None] + off[None, :], 0, n - 1)
    return sig[idx]  # (n, k)

# REAL semiring (+, *): one diffusion step -> smoothing
def step_real(sig):
    g = gather(sig, offsets)
    return (g * w_diffuse[None, :]).sum(axis=1)

# max-plus (max, +): dilation -> grows the object
def step_maxplus(sig):
    g = gather(sig, offsets)
    return (g + w_struct[None, :]).max(axis=1)

# min-plus (min, +): one relaxation of a distance transform.
# Seed: 0 inside the object, +inf outside; each step takes the min over
# neighbours + edge cost (=1), so values become distance-to-object.
INF = 1e9
def step_minplus(dist):
    g = gather(dist, offsets)
    cost = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 1.0])  # step to a neighbour costs 1, stay costs 0
    return (g + cost[None, :]).min(axis=1)

real = mask.copy()
for _ in range(8):
    real = step_real(real)

dil = mask.copy()
for _ in range(4):
    dil = step_maxplus(dil)

dist = np.where(mask > 0, 0.0, INF)
for _ in range(n):
    dist = step_minplus(dist)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(9, 6), sharex=True)
ax[0].step(x, mask, where="mid", color="0.5", label="object (mask)")
ax[0].plot(x, real, color="C0", lw=2, label="REAL $(+,\\times)$: diffusion / smoothing")
ax[0].legend(loc="upper right"); ax[0].set_ylabel("value")

ax[1].step(x, mask, where="mid", color="0.5", label="object (mask)")
ax[1].step(x, dil, where="mid", color="C3", lw=2, label="max-plus $(\\max,+)$: dilation")
ax[1].legend(loc="upper right"); ax[1].set_ylabel("value")

ax[2].step(x, mask, where="mid", color="0.5", label="object (mask)")
ax[2].plot(x, dist, color="C2", lw=2, label="min-plus $(\\min,+)$: distance transform")
ax[2].legend(loc="upper right"); ax[2].set_ylabel("distance")
ax[2].set_xlabel("grid index")

fig.suptitle("One operator machine, three semirings: swap $(\\oplus,\\otimes)$, change the geometry", y=0.98)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

In `nitrix` this machine is real code, not only a metaphor. The
[`nitrix.semiring`](operators.qmd#sec-semiring) package exports the algebras (`REAL`, `LOG`,
`TROPICAL_MAX_PLUS`, `TROPICAL_MIN_PLUS`, `BOOLEAN`, `EUCLIDEAN`) and the streaming reductions
(`semiring_matmul`, `semiring_ell_matmul`, `semiring_conv`) that consume them. The morphology
operators ([volumes](volumes.qmd#sec-morphology)) - `dilate`, `erode`, `open`, `close`,
`distance_transform` - are thin specialisations of `semiring_conv` over the tropical algebras, and
the sphere convolution `spherical_conv` is `REAL` re-backed on `semiring_ell_matmul` for $O(n \cdot
k)$ instead of $O(n^2)$. Learn the machine once on the [operators](operators.qmd) page; you will
recognise it everywhere after.

### Thread 3 — the Laplace–Beltrami operator {#sec-thread-lbo}

If the semiring machine is the engine of the *volume* side, the Laplace--Beltrami operator is the
engine of the *surface* side. On a smooth manifold $\Delta$ measures how much a function differs from
the average of its neighbourhood - it is the curved-space generalisation of the familiar $\nabla^2$.
Discretised on a mesh it factors into two pieces from the canonical notation: a stiffness matrix $L$
(the cotangent Laplacian, symmetric PSD, with edge weights $w_{ij} = \tfrac12(\cot \alpha_{ij} + \cot
\beta_{ij})$) and a mass matrix $M = \operatorname{diag}(A_i)$ (the lumped per-vertex areas), giving

$$
\Delta = M^{-1} L .
$$ {#eq-index-lbo}

Because $L$ and $M$ are built from edge angles and triangle areas - intrinsic quantities - $\Delta$
is invariant to rigid motion and to isometric bending (Thread 1 again); that intrinsicality is what
makes it the natural surface operator. That one operator unlocks three chapters. Run heat flow with
it and you get *smoothing*: `surface_smooth` diffuses a per-vertex scalar field - `sulc`, `curv`,
myelin, thickness - along the cortex with one backward-Euler step, solving the SPD system $(M +
tL)\,x = M x_0$ for diffusion time $t$. (Its combinatorial cousin `mesh_laplacian_smooth` smooths
vertex *positions* with the uniform 1-ring Laplacian instead of the cotangent one - a cheaper
geometric denoiser, not the metric-aware field smoother.) Apply $\Delta$ to the *coordinate*
functions and you read off *curvature*: mean curvature $H$ from $\Delta$ acting on the embedding,
Gaussian curvature $K$ from the angle defect, principal curvatures $\kappa_1 \ge \kappa_2$ from the
shape operator ([curvature](curvature.qmd)). Solve the generalised eigenproblem $L\phi_k = \lambda_k
M \phi_k$ and you get the *spectrum*: eigenfunctions $\phi_k$ and eigenvalues $\lambda_k$ that give a
shape-DNA descriptor, a heat kernel, and a spectral embedding ([spectral](spectral.qmd)). The pieces
are built in [Laplace--Beltrami](laplace-beltrami.qmd) (`mesh_cotangent_laplacian`,
`mesh_mass_matrix`); everything downstream reuses them.

The graph world mirrors this exactly: the [`nitrix.graph`](graph-geometry.qmd) package builds a
`laplacian` on an arbitrary weighted graph and reuses the same spectral toolkit (`laplacian_eigenmap`,
`diffusion_embedding`) for connectomes and parcellation - a mesh is, after all, a graph with geometry
attached.

### Thread 4 — device discipline {#sec-thread-device}

The fourth thread is not mathematics but engineering, and it explains the API's shape. `nitrix` runs
on GPUs through XLA, not Pallas/Triton, and deliberately avoids the vendor solver cuSOLVER (which is
unreliable on the accelerators we target). That forces a clean split, made precise in
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-classes), that we tag throughout the unit:

- **DIFF-JAX** - jittable *and* differentiable, fixed output shape: the operators you put inside
  training loops (the cotangent Laplacian apply, smoothing, the semiring matmuls).
- **HOST-CTOR** - data-dependent *construction* whose output shape you cannot know in advance (the
  icosphere builder, `marching_cubes`, `mesh_to_sdf`). It runs on the host and emits JAX arrays.
- **HOST-QA** - host-side *query / quality-assurance* that returns a Python scalar or does cleanup
  (`euler_characteristic`, `genus`, `find_self_intersections`).

The single rule that ties them together: never call a HOST op inside a jitted loop. Build your mesh
and run your QA gates on the host once; then hand the fixed-shape arrays to the DIFF-JAX operators for
the hot path. Every page tags its routines so you always know which side of that line you are on.

## Chapters {#sec-chapters}

Read in order for the full climb; each chapter assumes the ones before it.

1. [**Foundations**](foundations.qmd) - discretisation, transformations and inductive bias, the
   DIFF-JAX / HOST-CTOR / HOST-QA execution classes, device discipline, coordinate and array
   conventions, and how we validate (analytic oracle + correlation). *Read this first.*
2. [**Operators**](operators.qmd) - the ELL sparse format, the semiring machine, the `apply`
   interface, and adjacency: the substrate everything else runs on.
3. [**Laplace--Beltrami**](laplace-beltrami.qmd) - the cotangent stiffness $L$, the lumped mass $M$,
   and mesh smoothing: the surface engine.
4. [**Spectral geometry**](spectral.qmd) - the eigenproblem $L\phi_k = \lambda_k M \phi_k$, the heat
   kernel, and spectral embeddings.
5. [**Curvature**](curvature.qmd) - mean, Gaussian, and principal curvatures, and areal/strain
   distortion, validated against the sphere and Gauss--Bonnet.
6. [**Volumes**](volumes.qmd) - morphology, the exact Euclidean distance transform, and
   signed-distance fields: the semiring machine on grids.
7. [**Volume ↔ surface**](volume-surface.qmd) - `marching_cubes`, `mesh_to_sdf`, and the genus-0
   topology gate: the bridge between the two representations.

Technical-reference tier (referenced throughout, read as needed):

- [**Solvers**](solvers.qmd) - the cuSOLVER-free eigensolve, conjugate gradients, and the kernels
  behind them.
- [**Performance**](performance.qmd) - ELL / SectionedELL storage, padding, and the XLA backend.
- [**Graph geometry**](graph-geometry.qmd) - the graph Laplacian, graph pooling, and spectral graph
  methods, reusing the surface toolkit for connectomes.

## Scope: what this unit is, and is not {#sec-scope}

This unit is the geometry of objects and the operators on them - meshes, volumes, Laplacians,
curvature, distance, isosurfaces. It is *not* about maps between geometries: registration, resampling,
the Lie-group transform chart (`rigid_exp` / `affine_exp`), diffeomorphic velocity-field warps,
spherical parameterisation, and `surface_resample` all belong to a separate registration/deformation
unit, and parcellation/label transfer to another again. When this unit touches a map (the
volume-surface bridge, say) it does so only as a relationship between objects, never as a
registration problem.

## Reading paths {#sec-paths}

- **Fast path** - read each chapter's opening summary plus [foundations](foundations.qmd) and
  [operators](operators.qmd) in full. That is enough to use the unit and to know which operator
  answers which question.
- **Thorough path** - read in chapter order. Each chapter names the continuous object it
  approximates, proves convergence where it can, and validates against an analytic oracle.
- **Developer path** - follow the source links in each page's *See also* and lean on the reference
  tier ([solvers](solvers.qmd), [performance](performance.qmd), [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd))
  for storage layouts, kernel choices, and the execution-class contract.

## Further reading

- M. Botsch, L. Kobbelt, M. Pauly, P. Alliez, B. Lévy (2010). *Polygon Mesh Processing*. A K Peters /
  CRC Press. The standard reference for discrete mesh operators, the cotangent Laplacian, and
  remeshing.
- K. Crane, F. de Goes, M. Desbrun, P. Schröder (2013). *Digital Geometry Processing with Discrete
  Exterior Calculus*. ACM SIGGRAPH Courses. The discrete-approximates-smooth viewpoint developed
  rigorously.
- M. Meyer, M. Desbrun, P. Schröder, A. H. Barr (2003). "Discrete Differential-Geometry Operators for
  Triangulated 2-Manifolds." In *Visualization and Mathematics III*, Springer. The mixed-Voronoi area
  and cotangent operator.
- W. E. Lorensen, H. E. Cline (1987). "Marching Cubes: A High Resolution 3D Surface Construction
  Algorithm." *ACM SIGGRAPH Computer Graphics* 21(4). The original isosurface algorithm; we use the
  crack-free marching-tetrahedra variant.
- B. Lévy, R. H. Zhang (2010). "Spectral Mesh Processing." ACM SIGGRAPH Courses. The Laplace--Beltrami
  spectrum as a geometric signal-processing basis.
- M. M. Bronstein, J. Bruna, T. Cohen, P. Veličković (2021). *Geometric Deep Learning: Grids, Groups,
  Graphs, Geodesics, and Gauges*. arXiv:2104.13478. The Erlangen-programme view of invariance and
  equivariance behind Thread 1.
- F. Baccelli, G. Cohen, G. J. Olsder, J.-P. Quadrat (1992). *Synchronization and Linearity: An
  Algebra for Discrete Event Systems*. Wiley. The tropical (max-plus / min-plus) semiring foundations
  behind the operator machine.

## See also

- Package root: [`nitrix.geometry`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/__init__.py)
- The operator substrate: [`nitrix.semiring`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring/__init__.py)
  and [`nitrix.sparse`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/__init__.py)
- Grid operators: [`nitrix.morphology`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/morphology/__init__.py)
- The graph mirror: [`nitrix.graph`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/graph/__init__.py)
- Sibling unit: [the statistical modelling suite](../statistics/index.qmd).
